cot_text:
Okay, let's see. The question is about whether a man can legally leave all his money to his widow's sister, who he plans to marry next year, and then marry her. Hmm, so the key points here are the inheritance and the subsequent marriage.

First, I need to recall how inheritance laws work. In many jurisdictions, when someone dies, their assets are distributed according to their will or, if there's no will, by intestacy laws. If the man has a will, and in that will he names his widow's sister as the sole beneficiary, that's one thing. But the twist here is that this sister is someone he plans to marry next year. So, after he dies, she would inherit all his money, and then he would marry her. Wait, but if he's dead, how can he marry her? That doesn't make sense. Oh, wait, maybe the question is phrased differently: he leaves his money to his widow's sister, who he is going to marry in the future. So, he's alive when he makes the will, and the recipient is the sister of the current widow, and he's going to marry that sister next year. But then, if he dies before marrying, the sister becomes the new widow. But if he dies after marrying, then she's already his wife. But the will is made before the marriage. 

Wait, but the man is alive when he makes the will. So, his current wife is someone, and he's going to marry her sister next year. So, in his will, he leaves everything to the sister of his current wife. Then, after he dies, the sister inherits the money, and then later he marries her. Wait, but if he's dead, he can't marry her. So perhaps the phrasing is off. Maybe the man is alive now, with a wife (the "widow" in the question is a typo or misstatement—maybe "wife" or "future wife"?). Because a widow is a woman whose husband has died. So, if he's alive and plans to marry his future wife, who is the sister of his current widow. Wait, that doesn't make sense. Maybe the question is that he's currently married to a woman, and he's planning to marry her sister next year. So, he leaves all his money to his wife's sister, who he will marry after his current wife dies or after his own death. But the problem is, if he leaves money to his wife's sister in his will, and then after he dies, he can't marry her because he's dead. Unless the will is made before he dies, and he plans to marry her after his wife's death. Wait, maybe the man is alive, his current wife is alive, and he plans to marry the sister of his current wife after his current wife dies. So, he leaves his money to the sister in his will, and after his own death, he will have married her. But if he dies before marrying her, then the sister is the beneficiary. If he marries her before he dies, then she would be his wife, and the will might change. But the will is made before the marriage. So, the question is whether that's legally valid.

So, the key is whether a person can legally leave their assets to someone who, after the person's death, becomes their spouse. But in most legal systems, a person cannot inherit from their spouse if the spouse was the beneficiary of the will because the spouse is already a legal heir or because the will can be changed after marriage. Wait, but if the will is made before the marriage, then the spouse would not be the beneficiary if the will is made before the marriage. So, if the man makes a will that leaves everything to his wife's sister, and then later marries that sister, then after his death, his wife (now his sister-in-law before marriage, but after marriage his wife) would be the beneficiary of the will. Wait, no: if he marries the sister after making the will, then the will doesn't change unless he updates it. So, if he dies after marrying her, then in his will, he left everything to his wife's sister (who is now his wife). Wait, that's a contradiction. If he's married to her, then she's his wife, not his wife's sister. So, the will is made when she is the sister of his wife, but after they marry, she's his wife. So, the will would have to be amended to account for the change in relationship. If it's not amended, the will might still name her as the sister of his former wife, but now she's his wife. But the will may still stand unless it's revoked or changed. So, is that legal? 

In many jurisdictions, a will is a legal document that can be revoked or changed by the testator (the person making the will) until their death. Once the will is made, unless the testator gets married, that marriage might revoke the will in some jurisdictions. For example, in some places, if you get married after making a will, the will is automatically revoked unless it's made in contemplation of the marriage. But in this case, the man is planning to marry the sister of his current wife. So, if he makes a will leaving everything to his current wife's sister, and then later marries her, does the will get revoked? If so, then the will would be invalid, and the inheritance would go by intestacy laws (or to his new wife if she's an heir). However, if the marriage does not automatically revoke the will, then the sister (now his wife) would still be the beneficiary. 

But there's another aspect: if the man makes a will leaving everything to his wife's sister, and then marries her, the sister would be his wife, and in many legal systems, spouses are entitled to a portion of the inheritance even if not named in the will. So, even if the will says to leave everything to the sister, once she becomes his wife, she would have a legal right to inherit a portion, potentially overriding the will. So, the will might not be entirely valid in that scenario. 

Additionally, there's the issue of whether it's permissible for someone to leave their estate to a future spouse. Since the spouse doesn't exist at the time the will is made, the will can't specifically name them. However, the will can contain a provision that says "to the person I marry," but that's more complicated and might not be clear. In this case, the will is naming a specific person who is currently the sister of the spouse, but who may become the spouse later. 

Another angle is whether the man can marry his wife's sister. In some jurisdictions, marrying a sibling of one's spouse may be legally prohibited, but that's more about incest laws. If the sister is not a blood relative, then it's permissible. But if they are blood relatives, then it might be illegal. The question doesn't specify, so I have to assume it's legally permissible for them to marry. 

So, wrapping up: the man makes a will leaving everything to his wife's sister, who he plans to marry next year. If he dies before marrying her, the sister inherits as per the will. If he marries her first, then depending on the jurisdiction, the will might be revoked upon marriage, making the sister (now his wife) an heir by law rather than by the will. Alternatively, if the will is not revoked, then she would inherit as per the will, but then as a spouse, she might have additional rights. 

Therefore, the legality depends on the jurisdiction's laws regarding wills being revoked upon marriage, the rights of a spouse to inherit, and whether the will is amended after the marriage. The answer would likely be that it's not valid because the man cannot inherit from his spouse in most cases, so if he marries her after making the will, the will is either revoked or overridden by the spouse's legal inheritance rights.
